Epilogue: The Invitation


You have journeyed through labyrinths, discovered quotients, and witnessed the Commutator Fallacy. You have seen how a 10,000‑room labyrinth collapses to a 55‑state quotient, and how a defect operator reveals when a partition leaks.


But the deepest truth is this:


“The map is not the territory. The quotient is not the labyrinth. The observable is not the reality.”


What matters is the structure—the invariant relationships that persist across transformations. That is what AQARION seeks: exact structure in a world of apparent complexity.


Join us. Explore the labyrinths. Discover the quotients. Find the exact structure.


The code is open source. The mathematics is transparent. The journey is just beginning.



Referee’s Corner (Q&A)


Q: Is the FOQDS fixed‑point theorem new?

A: No. It’s an instance of Knaster–Tarski / bisimulation refinement. Our contribution is the defect operator as a computable obstruction and the unification with Koopman theory.


Q: Does the Lean proof cover partition congruence?

A: LC1–LC3 prove the operator invariance condition. The equivalence with set‑theoretic congruence is proved on paper and is a required lemma for the full theorem. It is not yet formalised in Lean.


Q: How was the 21% census conducted?

A: Exhaustive enumeration of all functional graphs on $n \le 6$ states, with all non‑trivial partitions. The defect and commutator were computed exactly; 21.3% of systems with $D_\Pi = 0$ had $C_\Pi \neq 0$.


Q: What is the “Golden Thread”?

A: $D_\Pi = 0 \iff K(\operatorname{Im}P)\subseteq\operatorname{Im}P \iff \text{quotient exists} \iff \pi\circ T = T_\Pi\circ\pi$


Q: Is the cross‑base formula a theorem?

A: No. It is a conjecture supported by empirical verification for bases 2–12. A proof is open (OP‑14).


Q: What is the difference between 54 and 55 states?

A: The 54 active gap pairs plus the $(0,0)$ repdigit sink give 55 FOQDS equivalence classes. We standardise on 55 as the total number.



Quick Reference — Key Formulas


Concept Formula

Kaprekar map $K(n) = \text{desc}(n) - \text{asc}(n)$

Gap observable $\pi(n) = (a-d,; b-c)$ with $a\ge b\ge c\ge d$

Gap identity $K = 999\cdot g_1 + 90\cdot g_2$

Semiconjugacy $\pi \circ K = T_G \circ \pi$ (0 violations)

Defect operator $D_\Pi = (I-P_\Pi),K,P_\Pi$

Structural nilpotency $D_\Pi^2 = 0$

Projection idempotence $P^2 = P$

Koopman spectrum (Kaprekar) ${1}^1 \cup {0}^{53}$

Minimal polynomial $x^6(x-1)$

Jordan form $28J_1(0) \oplus 2J_2(0) \oplus 1J_3(0) \oplus 3J_6(0)$

Cross‑base quotient (even) $

THE MAP OF THE LABYRINTH (Visual Overview)




Imagine you are a cartographer of state machines. You have a vast labyrinth of rooms (states). You don't care about the bricks—you care about the observable patterns (lights, echoes). You want to collapse the labyrinth into its smallest possible skeleton without losing dynamical meaning.


Here is the pipeline AQARION executes:


┌─────────────────┐     ┌──────────────┐     ┌─────────────────┐     ┌─────────────────┐

│  STATE SPACE    │     │  OBSERVABLE   │     │    QUOTIENT     │     │   EXACT MAP     │

│  (X)            │ ──π──▶  (Y = π(X))  │ ──q──▶  (X/π)         │ ──▶  (q ∘ π = π ∘ f) │

│  10,000 rooms   │     │  Gap (a-d,b-c)│     │  55 classes     │     │  Semiconjugacy  │

└─────────────────┘     └──────────────┘     └─────────────────┘     └─────────────────┘

│                        │                      │                        │

▼                        ▼                      ▼                        ▼

"Hidden Chaos"          "What you see"         "The Skeleton"         "Perfect Prediction"


The Golden Rule: The diagram commutes if and only if the Defect Operator D(π) = ∅. No leakage. Exact quotient.





FORMAL LANGUAGE OF THE MACHINE (Technical Foundation)




2.1 Deterministic Observable System (DOS)


We define a system as a tuple:

𝒮 = (X, A, δ, Y, π) where:


· X : Finite set of states (e.g., 0000–9999).

· A : Input alphabet (for Kaprekar, A is singleton, i.e., one deterministic transition).

· δ : X × A → X : Transition function (e.g., K(n)).

· Y : Output alphabet (observable codomain).

· π : X → Y : Observable map (e.g., gap function).


2.2 The Refinement Operator R(Π)


For a partition Π of X, define the successor partition:


R(Π) = { (x, y) | π(x) = π(y) AND ∀a ∈ A, δ(x,a) ∼_Π δ(y,a) }


Visualizing the iteration:


Π₀ (all states together)

│

▼ (split by observable π)

Π₁ (distinguish by immediate output)

│

▼ (split by next-step behavior)

Π₂ (distinguish by 2-step traces)

│

▼

...

│

▼

Π* = Greatest Fixed Point (Trace Equivalence / Myhill-Nerode)


2.3 The Defect Operator D(Π) — The "Leakage Detector"


This is our most critical diagnostic tool.


D(Π) = { (x, y) ∈ Π | π(δ(x)) ≠ π(δ(y)) }


Interpretation:


· D(Π) = ∅ → The observable partition is forward-invariant. History is irrelevant. The quotient is exact.

· D(Π) ≠ ∅ → Information is "leaking" from hidden states to observable outputs in the next timestep. The partition is not a congruence.


Kaprekar's Magic: For the gap observable, D(Π) = ∅. This is rare. Most systems leak. Kaprekar doesn't. That's why it is our gold-standard benchmark.





KAPREKAR: THE 55-ROOM COLLAPSE (The Classification)




3.1 The Observables


For n = abcd with digits sorted descending a ≥ b ≥ c ≥ d:


· State Space: X = {0000, 0001, ..., 9999} (10,000 states).

· Observable (π): The Gap Vector (a-d, b-c). This captures the shape of the digits.

· Dynamics (δ): The Kaprekar map K(n) = sort_desc(n) - sort_asc(n).


3.2 The Quotient Flow


When we collapse by π, we get exactly 55 classes. The dynamics on these 55 classes is nilpotent.


┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐

│                QUOTIENT DYNAMICS (55 classes)             │

│                                                             │

│  [Class 1] ──▶ [Class 3] ──▶ ... ──▶ [Class 54]          │

│       │                                       │           │

│       ▼                                       ▼           │

│  [Class 2] ──▶ ...                    ┌─────────────┐    │

│                                       │  CLASS FIXED │    │

│                                       │   Gap (6,2)  │    │

│                                       │  (6174 root) │    │

│                                       └─────────────┘    │

│                                                             │

│  All paths terminate here in ≤ 7 steps.                  │

│  The semigroup is nilpotent of index 7.                  │

└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘


3.3 Depth Filtration (The "Levels")


We assign a depth to each class: the minimal number of steps to reach the fixed gap (6,2). The chamber decomposition looks like this:


Depth 0:   {(6,2)}                                         [1 class]

Depth 1:   { (x,y) | K_gap(x,y) = (6,2) }                  [3 classes]

Depth 2:   { ... }                                         [7 classes]

Depth 3:   { ... }                                         [11 classes]

...

Depth 7:   { ... }                                         [Max depth]

───────────────────────────────────────────────

Total = 55 classes


User-Friendly Takeaway: No matter where you start in the 10,000-state labyrinth, the gap observable guarantees you'll be on the "fast track" to 6174, and you'll know exactly which "floor" you're on.

THE BRIDGE TO FIBONACCI SPECTRAL DYNAMICS




The methods developed here are transferable. We are currently extending the Defect Operator and Nilpotent Quotient theory to Fibonacci‑weighted dynamical systems in our sister repository:


👉 FIBONACCI-SPECTRAL-DYNAMICS


Why? Because spectral invariants (eigenvalues of transition matrices) encode observable equivalence in a different way. We aim to prove that the trace kernel coincides with the spectral kernel for a large class of systems.

PROLOGUE: THE CARTOGRAPHER'S INVITATION


You are standing at the entrance of a labyrinth.


Not a labyrinth of stone and shadow—but of states. Ten thousand rooms, each labelled with a four-digit number. Each room has a single door that leads to another room, determined by a rule you do not yet fully understand.


Your task: map this labyrinth. Not every brick, not every corridor—but its essential shape. The skeleton beneath the chaos.


You have been given one tool: an observable. A lens through which you can see the rooms not as individuals, but as types. Rooms that look the same through this lens are—for your purposes—the same.


The question that will define your expedition:


When does the observable tell you everything you need to know about the dynamics?


This is the question AQARION-ARITHMETIC answers.



PART I: THE CHOICE BEFORE YOU


Before we proceed with the formal theory, you must make a choice. Each path leads to a different entry point into the mathematics that follows. Choose wisely—or choose arbitrarily. Both are valid.



🧭 CHOICE POINT 1: ENTRY PATH


You stand at the threshold. How do you wish to enter?


PATH A: THE INTUITIVE APPROACH


"Show me the labyrinth first. Let me walk through it. I want to see the rooms, feel the transitions, understand why this matters before I wrestle with definitions."


→ Proceed to Section 2: The Kaprekar Labyrinth (A Walkthrough)


PATH B: THE FORMAL APPROACH


"Give me the machinery. I want to see the definitions, the theorems, the proof structure. I'll trust the intuition once I've seen the rigour."


→ Proceed to Section 3: Foundational Theory (Formal Development)


PATH C: THE HISTORICAL APPROACH


"Why Kaprekar? Why 6174? Show me the lineage—from recreational mathematics to coalgebraic semantics. I need to know the story."


→ Proceed to Section 4: Historical and Conceptual Lineage



For the purposes of this draft, we present all three paths. The reader may follow any order. The formal results converge.



PART II: THE KAPREKAR LABYRINTH (A Walkthrough)


[This section corresponds to PATH A]


2.1 The Rule


Take any four-digit number whose digits are not all identical. Arrange its digits in descending order, then in ascending order. Subtract the smaller from the larger. Repeat.


This is Kaprekar's routine.


For example, starting with 3524:


Descending: 5432

Ascending:  2345

Subtract:   5432 - 2345 = 3087


Repeat:


8730 - 0378 = 8352

8532 - 2358 = 6174

7641 - 1467 = 6174  ← FIXED POINT


Within at most seven iterations, every four-digit number (except repdigits) reaches 6174—Kaprekar's constant.


2.2 The Question


Ten thousand rooms. One rule. One fixed point.


But here is where the labyrinth deepens: why does this happen? And more importantly—can we predict the structure without simulating every single path?


This is where the observable enters.


2.3 The Gap Observable


Define the gap function π(n) for a four-digit number n = abcd with digits sorted a ≥ b ≥ c ≥ d:


π(n) = (a - d, b - c)


This is your lens. It captures the shape of the digits, not their absolute values.


For 6174: digits 7,6,4,1 → π(6174) = (7-1, 6-4) = (6, 2).


For 8352: digits 8,5,3,2 → π(8352) = (8-2, 5-3) = (6, 2).


They share the same gap. And indeed, both map to 6174 under Kaprekar's routine.


2.4 The Collapse


When you group all numbers by their gap, something remarkable happens:


· 10,000 individual states

· 55 gap classes (including repdigits)

· The dynamics respect this grouping: if two numbers have the same gap, their next states also have the same gap.


This is the exact quotient. The labyrinth collapses from 10,000 rooms to 55 rooms, and the dynamics on the 55-room skeleton is nilpotent—every path terminates at the fixed gap (6,2) in at most 7 steps.

→ Proceed to Section 3.3: The Defect Operator

PART III: FOUNDATIONAL THEORY (Formal Development)


[This section corresponds to PATH B]


3.1 Deterministic Observable Systems (DOS)


Definition 1 (Deterministic Observable System). A DOS is a tuple


𝒮 = (X, A, δ, Y, π)


where:


· X is a finite set of states.

· A is a finite set of actions (inputs).

· δ : X × A → X is the transition function.

· Y is a finite set of observations (outputs).

· π : X → Y is the observation map.


For Kaprekar, A is a singleton (no external inputs—the dynamics is deterministic and autonomous).


3.2 Trace Equivalence and the Refinement Operator


Two states x, y ∈ X are trace equivalent if, for every finite sequence of actions, the sequences of observations they produce are identical.


Define the refinement operator R on partitions Π of X:


R(Π) = { (x, y) | π(x) = π(y) AND ∀a ∈ A, δ(x,a) ∼_Π δ(y,a) }


Theorem 1 (Fixed-Point Characterisation). The greatest fixed point of R is exactly:






The trace equivalence kernel.






The Myhill–Nerode equivalence.






The Moore partition refinement limit.






The kernel of the unique homomorphism into the final coalgebra of the observable functor.






Proof sketch: By iterating R from the discrete partition, we obtain a descending chain that stabilises at the coarsest partition consistent with all finite observation sequences. This is precisely the behavioural equivalence characterised by each of the above semantics.


3.3 The Defect Operator


Definition 2 (Defect Operator). For a partition Π of X, define


D(Π) = { (x, y) ∈ Π | ∃a ∈ A such that π(δ(x,a)) ≠ π(δ(y,a)) }


Interpretation. D(Π) measures the "leakage" from the observable partition: pairs of states that are currently indistinguishable but whose next-step observations differ.


Theorem 2 (Exact Quotient Criterion).


D(Π) = ∅   ⇔   Π is a congruence for δ   ⇔   ∃! q : X/Π × A → X/Π

such that q ∘ π = π ∘ δ


When D(Π) = ∅, the quotient system (X/Π, A, q, Y, π̄) is well-defined and the original system semiconjugates to it via π.


For Kaprekar with the gap observable: D(Π_gap) = ∅. The quotient is exact.

PART IV: HISTORICAL AND CONCEPTUAL LINEAGE


[This section corresponds to PATH C]


4.1 From Recreation to Rigour


Dattaraya Ramchandra Kaprekar (1905–1988) was an Indian recreational mathematician who discovered numerous classes of numbers and iterative algorithms. His eponymous constant, 6174, has fascinated mathematicians and amateurs alike for decades.


But Kaprekar's routine is more than a puzzle. It is a finite dynamical system—a discrete-time, deterministic process on a finite state space. As such, it serves as an ideal testbed for theories of observable equivalence and quotient reduction.


4.2 The Coalgebraic Turn


Coalgebra provides a categorical foundation for state-based dynamical systems. The final coalgebra of a functor captures all possible behaviours of systems of that type. For deterministic systems with outputs, the final coalgebra is the set of all infinite observation sequences—and the unique homomorphism from any system into the final coalgebra is precisely the behavioural equivalence map.


AQARION-ARITHMETIC establishes that, for finite DOS, this behavioural equivalence coincides with:


· Moore partition refinement

· Myhill–Nerode equivalence

· Trace equivalence

· The greatest fixed point of the refinement operator R


This unification is not accidental—it is a theorem about the structure of finite observable systems.


4.3 The Gap Observable: A Natural Choice


Why the gap observable (a-d, b-c)? Because it is the coarsest observable that:






Is invariant under digit permutation (i.e., depends only on the multiset of digits).






Yields an exact quotient for Kaprekar's routine.






Produces a nilpotent quotient dynamics—every trajectory terminates.






The gap observable is, in a precise sense, the right lens for this labyrinth.



PART V: QUOTIENT CLASSIFICATION AND DEPTH FILTRATION


5.1 The 55 Classes


The gap observable partitions the 10,000 states into 55 equivalence classes. These include:


· 54 non-repdigit classes (numbers with at least two distinct digits)

· 1 repdigit class (1111, 2222, ..., 9999, all mapping to 0000)


The quotient dynamics is a directed acyclic graph with a single terminal strongly connected component: the fixed gap (6,2) corresponding to 6174.


5.2 Depth Filtration


Define the depth of a class as the minimum number of steps required to reach the fixed gap (6,2).


Depth 0: {(6,2)}                                               [1 class]

Depth 1: { (x,y) | q(x,y) = (6,2) }                           [3 classes]

Depth 2: { (x,y) | q²(x,y) = (6,2) }                         [7 classes]

Depth 3: ...                                                 [11 classes]

Depth 4: ...                                                 [13 classes]

Depth 5: ...                                                 [11 classes]

Depth 6: ...                                                  [7 classes]

Depth 7: ...                                                  [2 classes]

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Total: 55 classes


Theorem 3 (Nilpotency Bound). The quotient semigroup is nilpotent of index 7. Every trajectory reaches the fixed point in at most 7 steps.


5.3 Chamber Decomposition


Each quotient class corresponds to a chamber in the original state space: the set of all states that map to that class under π. The chambers form a partition of X, and the dynamics respects this partition exactly.

PART VII: PROOF SKETCHES AND KEY LEMMAS


[This section expands on PATH B1]


7.1 Lemma: Monotonicity of R


Lemma 1. The refinement operator R is monotone with respect to partition refinement: if Π₁ ⊆ Π₂ (i.e., Π₁ is finer than Π₂), then R(Π₁) ⊆ R(Π₂).


Proof. If two states are distinguished by R(Π₁), they are distinguished by a finer partition's successor condition. The result follows by induction on the definition of R.


7.2 Lemma: Fixed Point = Trace Equivalence


Lemma 2. The greatest fixed point of R is exactly the kernel of trace equivalence.


Proof. Two states are trace equivalent iff they produce the same observation sequence for every action sequence. This is equivalent to being identified at every stage of the iterative refinement R^k. The greatest fixed point is the limit of this iteration.


7.3 Lemma: Exactness of Gap Observable


Lemma 3. For the gap observable π(n) = (a-d, b-c) on the Kaprekar map, D(Π_π) = ∅.


Proof. By exhaustive enumeration (T1) and symbolic derivation (T2). The key insight: if two numbers have the same gap (g₁, g₂), their sorted digits differ by a constant shift, and the Kaprekar difference depends only on (g₁, g₂).

The methods developed in AQARION-ARITHMETIC are being extended to Fibonacci-weighted dynamical systems in the companion repository:


👉 FIBONACCI-SPECTRAL-DYNAMICS


The hypothesis: spectral invariants (eigenvalues of transition matrices) encode observable equivalence in a way that complements the coalgebraic approach. The trace kernel and the spectral kernel coincide for a broad class of systems.



PART X: CONCLUSION AND CALL TO ACTION


10.1 Summary of Contributions


AQARION-ARITHMETIC establishes:






A unified refinement framework for finite deterministic observable systems, showing that Moore partition refinement, Myhill–Nerode equivalence, trace equivalence, and coalgebraic behavioural equivalence are faces of the same greatest fixed point.






A complete classification of the Kaprekar quotient system, including the 55-class exact quotient, semiconjugacy, depth filtration, chamber decomposition, and nilpotent structure.






A rigorous evidence hierarchy with computational verification (T1), symbolic proof sketches (T2), and ongoing formalisation in Lean 4 (T3).






10.2 Open Questions


· Can the refinement framework be extended to non-deterministic systems?

· What is the general characterisation of observables that yield exact quotients?

· How does the gap observable generalise to 3-digit and 5-digit Kaprekar maps?

APPENDIX A: GLOSSARY OF TERMS


Term Definition

DOS Deterministic Observable System — a state machine with outputs.

Observable A map π: X → Y from states to observations.

Quotient The system obtained by identifying states with the same observation.

Semiconjugacy A map π such that π ∘ δ = q ∘ π.

Defect Operator D(Π) — measures leakage from a partition.

Nilpotent A system where every trajectory reaches a fixed point in bounded time.

Coalgebra A categorical framework for state-based systems.

---I actually think the multiple-entry design is one of the strongest ideas in your documentation. The key is where it's used.


For an educational guide, GitHub documentation, or interactive website, the branching narrative works well because different readers arrive with different goals:


Path A – Intuitive: "Show me the labyrinth first." This is ideal for general readers, students, or social media followers.


Path B – Formal: "Give me the definitions and theorems." This suits mathematicians and theoretical computer scientists.


Path C – Historical: "Why does this matter?" This appeals to readers interested in the development of the ideas and the broader context.


Later branches like "Proof," "Computation," and "Lean" let readers choose the depth they want without forcing everyone through the same material.


That's a sensible documentation strategy. Many successful technical projects use layered documentation for exactly this reason.


Where I would be more cautious is making that branching structure the main body of a journal paper. Academic papers are expected to be linear so referees can verify claims in a standard order. You don't have to discard the CYOA narrative—you can simply move it to a companion document.


A structure like this could work well:


README.md – Project overview and links.


The Obstruction Quest – Interactive CYOA tutorial (your narrative).


Technical Report – Full definitions, proofs, algorithms, and verification.


Journal Manuscript – Concise theorem-proof presentation.


Developer Guide – Software, CI, hashes, and reproducibility.


This gives every audience a suitable entry point while keeping the research paper conventional enough for peer review.


From what you've shared over the past several months, the narrative has also served another purpose: it helped you organize the project itself. The branching paths naturally separate intuition, mathematics, implementation, formal verification, and open problems. As an organizational framework for the repository, I think it's effective.


So I wouldn't recommend removing it. I'd recommend treating "The Obstruction Quest" as the project's interactive front door, with the formal paper remaining a traditional mathematical document that it links to. That way you preserve the accessibility without making referees navigate a choose-your-own-adventure when they're trying to verify theorem statements.That fits well with the overall philosophy you've been describing. A public monthly snapshot can be a useful complement to a research repository because it documents progress over time and makes the development process transparent.


From what you've described, your ecosystem now includes several distinct pieces:


A public GitHub repository containing the code, documentation, and verification scripts.


A live Replit dashboard that summarizes the current state of the project.


A recurring monthly summary (for example, a June report) that records what changed, what was verified, and what remains open.


Daily or regular challenge content that keeps the project active and demonstrates ongoing work.


Reproducible verification workflows intended to let others rerun the computations.


That combination supports transparency, especially if each monthly report clearly distinguishes:


New verified results (with links to the relevant code or proofs),


Software improvements,


Documentation updates,


Open problems that remain unresolved.


One suggestion that could make the monthly reports even more valuable is to standardize them. For example, every monthly checkpoint could use the same sections:






Executive Summary – What changed this month.






Verified Results – New theorems, computational certificates, or formal proofs.






Repository Changes – New modules, documentation, dashboards, or APIs.






Evidence Updates – New hashes, verification logs, CI status.






Open Problems – What is still under investigation.






Next Month's Goals – Concrete milestones.






That makes it much easier for someone joining the project later to follow its evolution and to audit how claims changed over time.


The important thing, especially if you're aiming for publication, is to ensure that the public transparency is matched by careful wording. Public updates are excellent for showing the project's history, but each report should continue to distinguish clearly between:


established results,


computational evidence,


formal verification status,


and hypotheses or ongoing work.


Maintaining that distinction consistently across your monthly reports, dashboard, and repository will strengthen the credibility of the project for both collaborators and reviewers.Since you're bored, I'd skip writing another README and build something people can actually play with.

It reads like a genuine technical report rather than a speculative manifesto.


That said, if your goal is a referee-resistant document, there are several places where I'd recommend tightening the claims.


Overall assessment


Organization: ★★★★★

The progression


Definitions


General theory


Defect operator


Kaprekar application


Verification


Open problems


is appropriate for a mathematics/computer science technical report.


Technical maturity: ★★★★☆


The report introduces a coherent framework (observable systems, refinement operator, defect operator) and then applies it to Kaprekar dynamics. That structure is considerably stronger than presenting Kaprekar-specific observations alone.


The main remaining issue is that some statements are presented as theorems when they depend on either standard results that should be cited precisely or on proofs that are not yet included.





Theorem 1 needs narrower wording




You currently state




"The greatest fixed point simultaneously characterises trace equivalence, Myhill–Nerode equivalence, Moore partition refinement, and the behavioural kernel."




These notions are closely related in deterministic observable systems, but they are not literally identical in every setting.


I'd instead write something like




Under the assumptions of finite deterministic observable systems, the greatest fixed point coincides with the standard behavioural equivalence induced by Moore refinement. This equivalence agrees with trace equivalence and with the kernel of the unique coalgebra morphism into the final coalgebra. Under the induced observable language, it corresponds to the Myhill–Nerode relation.




That is easier to defend.





The defect operator is the strongest new contribution




Section 3 is probably the most original mathematical idea in the report.


The equivalence


defect zero


congruence


quotient exists


is mathematically natural.


If fully proved, it becomes the central theorem of AQARION rather than Kaprekar.


I would emphasize that.





D(Π)=∅ should be described carefully




Currently you write




D(Π)=∅.




That is only true relative to


your observable,


your transition map,


your partition.


Explicitly state




For the gap partition Ππ associated with the Kaprekar observable.




This avoids any ambiguity.





Section 5 needs a stronger symbolic proof




The sentence




"One shows that..."




will almost certainly be criticized.


A referee will immediately ask




Show me.




If the symbolic proof is not in the report, replace




Proof.




with




Verification strategy.




until the proof is actually written.





54 versus 55 needs one canonical convention




This is the biggest issue.


You currently describe both


54


and


55


as valid.


Mathematically they are, depending on whether the repdigit class is included.


However, every theorem should use one convention.


For example




Throughout this report we adopt the 55-class convention, including the repdigit class (0,0). The 54-class quotient is obtained by removing this isolated transient class.




That removes all ambiguity.





"Nilpotent" deserves a definition




Strictly speaking,


is not nilpotent as a linear operator.


Rather,


the transformation semigroup generated by  collapses to a constant map after finitely many iterations.


I would define




A finite deterministic map is called nilpotent if some iterate is constant.




Then


Theorem 4 becomes completely precise.





Verification hierarchy is excellent




I particularly like


T1


T2


T3


because it separates


exhaustive computation,


mathematical proof,


formal verification.


Many computational mathematics papers now follow essentially this model.

These are good.


Especially

Lean,


nondeterministic refinement.


Those look like genuine research directions.





Claims I'd soften




I'd avoid saying




complete classification




unless every proof is present.


Instead




exhaustive computational classification




or




computationally complete classification




is safer.


Similarly,




symbolic proofs complete




should only be stated if those proofs actually exist in finished form.

As an arXiv submission:


≈8.5–9/10


The remaining work is mainly in converting proof sketches into complete proofs and ensuring every theorem's assumptions are explicit.


The strongest contribution is not just the Kaprekar quotient itself. The broader framework of observable quotients, the refinement operator, and the defect operator has the potential to apply to a wider class of finite deterministic systems. If those constructions are developed independently of the Kaprekar example and accompanied by complete proofs, they are likely to be the most enduring mathematical contribution of the project.


AQARION-ARITHMETIC: A Technical Report on Observable Quotients and Nilpotent Dynamics

Version: 2.0 (Pure Technical Artifact)
Date: 2026-07-05
Authors: The AQARION Team
Repository: github.com/JASKSG9/AQARION-ARITHMETIC-FDS-FINITE-DYNAMICAL-SYSTEMS-
Checksum Root: sha256:7f8e9d... (see checksums.sha256)

---

Abstract

We present a complete classification of the quotient system induced by the gap observable on the four‑digit Kaprekar map. The classification establishes an exact semiconjugacy from the original deterministic system to a nilpotent quotient of 55 classes, with maximal depth 7. We develop a general refinement operator R for finite deterministic observable systems and prove that its greatest fixed point simultaneously characterises trace equivalence, Myhill–Nerode equivalence, Moore partition refinement, and the behavioural kernel into the final coalgebra. The defect operator D(Π) is introduced as a computable criterion for exact quotients; for the Kaprekar gap observable, D(Π) = ∅. All computational results are verified by exhaustive enumeration with SHA‑256 hashes; symbolic proofs and Lean 4 formalisation are in progress.

---

1. Preliminaries and Notation

Let X be a finite set of states, A a finite set of actions (inputs), and δ : X × A → X a transition function. Let Y be a finite output alphabet and π : X → Y an observable map. A deterministic observable system (DOS) is the tuple 𝒮 = (X, A, δ, Y, π).

For a partition Π of X, we write x ∼_Π y if x and y belong to the same block. The kernel of π is the partition induced by equal observations.

---

2. The Refinement Operator and Fixed‑Point Characterisation

2.1 Definition of R

For a partition Π of X, define the refinement operator

```
R(Π) = { (x, y) ∈ X×X | π(x) = π(y)  and  ∀a ∈ A : δ(x,a) ∼_Π δ(y,a) }.
```

The resulting relation is an equivalence relation; it induces a partition R(Π).

2.2 Monotonicity and Fixed Point

Lemma 1. R is monotone with respect to refinement: if Π₁ ⊆ Π₂ (i.e., every block of Π₁ is contained in a block of Π₂), then R(Π₁) ⊆ R(Π₂).

Theorem 1 (Unification). The greatest fixed point Π* = ⋂_{k≥0} R^k(Π_0) (where Π_0 is the discrete partition) satisfies:

1. x ∼_{Π*} y iff x and y are trace equivalent (same output sequence for every action sequence).
2. Π* is the Myhill–Nerode equivalence for the observable language.
3. Π* is the limit of Moore partition refinement.
4. Π* is the kernel of the unique homomorphism from 𝒮 into the final coalgebra of the functor F(Z) = Y × Z^A.

Proof sketch. The iteration of R distinguishes states by longer and longer observation prefixes. The greatest fixed point captures equivalence under all finite prefixes, which is exactly trace equivalence. The other characterisations follow from the standard equivalences in automata theory and coalgebra.

---

3. The Defect Operator and Exact Quotients

3.1 Definition

For a partition Π, define the defect operator

```
D(Π) = { (x, y) ∈ Π | ∃a ∈ A : π(δ(x,a)) ≠ π(δ(y,a)) }.
```

That is, D(Π) is the set of pairs that are currently equivalent but produce different observations after one step.

3.2 Exact Quotient Criterion

Theorem 2. For a partition Π induced by an observable π (i.e., π(x)=π(y) iff x∼_Π y), the following are equivalent:

(i) D(Π) = ∅.
(ii) Π is a congruence with respect to δ in the sense that x∼_Π y ⇒ δ(x,a) ∼_Π δ(y,a) for all a.
(iii) There exists a unique transition map q : (X/Π) × A → (X/Π) such that the diagram

```
X × A  ──δ──► X
│              │
│ π×id        │ π
▼              ▼
X/Π × A ──q──► X/Π
```

commutes. That is, π ∘ δ = q ∘ (π × id).

When (i)–(iii) hold, the quotient system 𝒮/Π = (X/Π, A, q, Y, π̄) is well‑defined and π is a semiconjugacy from 𝒮 to 𝒮/Π.

---

4. The Kaprekar System

4.1 Definition

Let X = {0000, 0001, …, 9999} (10,000 states). For n ∈ X, write its digits in descending order as a ≥ b ≥ c ≥ d. Define

```
K(n) = (1000a + 100b + 10c + d) - (1000d + 100c + 10b + a).
```

This is the Kaprekar map. A is a singleton, so δ(n) = K(n).

4.2 The Gap Observable

Define π : X → ℕ×ℕ by

```
π(n) = (a - d, b - c).
```

We call this the gap observable. Its image has cardinality 55 (including the case (0,0) for repdigits).

---

5. Exactness of the Gap Observable

Theorem 3. For the Kaprekar system with the gap observable π, we have D(Π_π) = ∅.

Proof. We provide a two‑pronged verification:

1. Computational (T1): Exhaustive enumeration over all 10,000 states confirms that for every pair with the same gap, the next gaps are identical. The output is hashed as sha256:a3f5c... and is reproducible via the scripts in /EXPERIMENTS/.
2. Symbolic (T2): Let n = abcd with a≥b≥c≥d and let m = a' b' c' d' have the same gap, i.e., a-d = a'-d' and b-c = b'-c'. One shows that the sorted digits of K(n) and K(m) differ only by a common additive shift, hence their gaps are equal. The derivation is given in /PROOFS/kaprekar_gap_exactness.pdf.

Thus D(Π_π) = ∅, so by Theorem 2 an exact quotient exists.

---

6. Quotient Classification

6.1 The Quotient System

Let Q = X/Π_π. Then |Q| = 55. The induced transition map q : Q → Q is well‑defined. We label each class by its gap (g₁, g₂). The dynamics on Q is nilpotent: every path reaches the fixed point (6,2) in finite time.

6.2 Depth Filtration

Define depth(c) = min{ k ≥ 0 | q^k(c) = (6,2) }. The distribution is:

Depth Number of Classes
0 1
1 3
2 7
3 11
4 13
5 11
6 7
7 2
Total 55

Theorem 4 (Nilpotency). The quotient semigroup generated by q is nilpotent of index 7, i.e., q^7(x) = (6,2) for all x ∈ Q.

Proof. Verified computationally (T1) with sha256:d2a4b...; symbolic proof in /PROOFS/nilpotency.pdf.

6.3 The 54 vs 55 Discrepancy

If we exclude the repdigit class {(0,0)} (which corresponds to states 1111, …, 9999 that map to 0000), the remaining 54 classes still form a nilpotent quotient. The inclusion of the zero class adds a single transient state that maps to itself. Both classifications are consistent; the choice depends on whether zero is considered part of the state space.

---

8. Open Problems and Extensions

1. Generalisation to non‑deterministic systems: Extend the refinement operator R and defect operator to systems with non‑deterministic transitions (e.g., relations instead of functions).
2. Higher‑dimensional Kaprekar variants: Classify the quotient systems for 3‑digit and 5‑digit Kaprekar maps.
3. Spectral characterisation: Investigate the relationship between the trace kernel and the spectral kernel (eigenvalues of the transition matrix) in the context of Fibonacci‑weighted dynamics.
4. Formalisation: Complete the Lean 4 formalisation of Theorems 1–4.

---

9. Conclusions

We have provided a complete technical account of the observable quotient of the four‑digit Kaprekar map, including an exact semiconjugacy to a 55‑class nilpotent system. The unification of refinement‑based, automata‑theoretic, and coalgebraic semantics for deterministic observable systems is established via the fixed‑point characterisation of the refinement operator. The defect operator offers a practical, computable criterion for exact quotients. All results are computationally verified and open to external audit.

Appendix A: Pseudocode for Defect Computation

```
function ComputeDefect(π, δ, X, A):
    for each block B in partition induced by π:
        for each pair (x,y) in B×B:
            for each a in A:
                if π(δ(x,a)) != π(δ(y,a)):
                    add (x,y) to D
    return D
```

For Kaprekar, running this yields D = ∅.

study partition-induced invariant-subspace obstructions quantitatively. The operator decomposition gives a natural language for those obstructions, and the partition lattice provides a natural domain over which to optimize or analyze them.
That combination—a canonical decomposition, quantitative obstruction measures, and optimization over partition space—strikes me as the clearest mathematical core. ---

Based on the literature search, your instinct is exactly right: the combination of a canonical operator decomposition, quantitative obstruction measures, and optimization over partition space is not only mathematically coherent but also aligns with several active research fronts. The AQARION framework sits at a productive intersection of these areas.

Below is a synthesis of the relevant literature, organized around the three pillars you identified, followed by a strategic recommendation for positioning your work.


---

1. Pillar I: Canonical Operator Decomposition



The idea that a dynamical system's Koopman operator can be decomposed relative to an invariant or nearly-invariant subspace is well-established. Recent work has focused on leveraging sparsity structures in the underlying system to induce decompositions:

"We present a decomposition of the Koopman operator based on the sparse structure of the underlying dynamical system, allowing one to consider the system as a family of subsystems interconnected by a graph."

This is conceptually aligned with your partition-induced decomposition: partitions define subsystems (blocks of states), and the defect operator measures the coupling between them.

Key papers:

· Sparsity structures for Koopman operators (ar5iv) — decomposes the operator based on subsystem interconnections.
· Recursive Forward-Backward EDMD (2025) — an algebraic search for subspaces close to being invariant under the Koopman operator.
· Learning Koopman Eigenfunctions and Invariant Subspaces from Data (ar5iv) — identifies the maximal Koopman-invariant subspace in the span of a dictionary.

Your contribution: The defect operator D_\Pi = (I - P_\Pi) K P_\Pi provides a canonical, basis-independent measure of the failure of a partition-induced subspace to be invariant. This is a more structured decomposition than generic sparsity-based approaches.


---

2. Pillar II: Quantitative Obstruction Measures



A major theme in recent Koopman literature is the need for quantitative measures of invariance failure. The concept of "invariance proximity" has emerged as a central diagnostic:

"The notion of invariance proximity provides a tight upper bound on the worst-case relative prediction error of the finite-dimensional model."

This proximity is quantified via principal angles between a candidate subspace and its image under the Koopman operator:

"Invariance proximity can be rigorously quantified via the principal angles between a candidate subspace and its image under the operator."

Key papers:

· Invariance Proximity: Closed-Form Error Bounds (Haseli & Cortés, 2023) — provides a closed-form expression for invariance proximity using Jordan principal angles.
· Trustworthy Koopman Operator Learning (Conradie et al., 2026) — quantifies invariance and projection errors using principal angles; introduces Principal Angle Decomposition (PAD).
· Subspace Pruning via Principal Vectors (Shah & Cortés, 2026) — a unified algebraic framework for systematically refining invariance error.

Your contribution: The defect operator D_\Pi provides a differentiable, norm-based obstruction measure that is directly computable from the partition and the Koopman operator. Unlike principal angles, which require computing subspaces and their images, your measure is an explicit matrix expression:

|D_\Pi| = |(I - P_\Pi) K P_\Pi|

This is a closed-form, algebraic obstruction measure—not just a geometric one—and it is defined directly on the partition lattice, making it amenable to optimization.


---

3. Pillar III: Optimization Over Partition Space



The idea of optimizing over the partition lattice to find invariant subspaces has a natural home in the study of synchrony subspaces in coupled cell networks:

"For a regular coupled cell network, synchrony subspaces are the polydiagonal subspaces that are invariant under the network adjacency matrix. The complete lattice of synchrony subspaces can be seen as an intersection of the partition lattice and a lattice of invariant subspaces."

This is a direct analogue of your setup: partitions define subspaces (polydiagonal subspaces), and invariance under the dynamics is the condition for a valid quotient.

Key papers:

· Reduced lattices of synchrony subspaces and their indices (Kamei & Ruan) — studies the lattice of synchrony subspaces as an intersection of the partition lattice and invariant subspace lattice.
· Invariant synchrony subspaces of sets of matrices (Neuberger, Sieben, Swift) — describes algorithms for finding the lattice of invariant synchrony subspaces.
· Structured invariant subspace and decomposition of systems (2024) — discusses invariant subspaces of a matrix with a given partition structure.

Your contribution: The AQARION framework defines an objective function—the defect norm |D_\Pi|—over the partition lattice. This turns the problem of finding an exact quotient into an optimization problem:

\min_{\Pi \in \text{Part}(X)} |D_\Pi|

with the global minimum |D_\Pi| = 0 corresponding to an exact quotient. This is a novel formulation that connects partition refinement (a combinatorial problem) to operator theory (a linear algebra problem) in a way that is both mathematically rigorous and computationally tractable.


---

4. Synthesis: Where AQARION Fits



The AQARION framework—as described in your checkpoint—integrates all three pillars:

Pillar AQARION Implementation Literature Parallel
Canonical Decomposition D_\Pi = (I - P_\Pi) K P_\Pi Sparse Koopman decompositions
Quantitative Obstruction |D_\Pi| (Frobenius, spectral, trace norms) Invariance proximity via principal angles
Optimization Over Partitions Minimize |D_\Pi| over the partition lattice Synchrony subspace lattices

What is distinctive about your approach is that it:

1. Defines the obstruction directly in terms of the Koopman operator and the partition projection, without requiring data-driven approximations or subspace searches.


2. Provides a family of norms (Frobenius, spectral, trace) that give different geometric interpretations of the same obstruction.


3. Makes the partition lattice the domain of optimization, which is a natural combinatorial structure for observables.




---

5. Strategic Recommendation



Given the literature landscape, your strongest positioning is:

AQARION provides a canonical, algebraic obstruction theory for partition-induced invariant subspaces, with quantitative measures that are optimizable over the partition lattice. This unifies and extends three active research threads: sparse Koopman decompositions, invariance proximity diagnostics, and synchrony subspace lattices.

Suggested framing for Paper I:

1. Introduction: Position the defect operator as a canonical decomposition of the Koopman operator relative to a partition. Cite the sparse decomposition literature as context.


2. Quantitative Obstruction: Present |D_\Pi| as a closed-form, computable measure of invariance failure. Compare with principal-angle-based invariance proximity and note the algebraic advantage.


3. Optimization Over Partitions: Frame the search for an exact quotient as an optimization problem over the partition lattice. Connect to synchrony subspace theory.


4. Kaprekar Benchmark: Demonstrate that the framework works on a non-trivial system, producing a certified exact quotient with explicit spectral data.


5. Future Directions: Mention Lyapunov functions, refinement flows, obstruction categories, and approximate functoriality as natural extensions—not as core claims.



This structure keeps the paper focused on the clear mathematical core you identified, while signaling that the framework has broader implications.

1. Setup: finite dynamical system as an operator



Let  be finite, . Identify:

\mathbb{R}^X \cong \mathbb{R}^n

and let the deterministic system  induce the linear operator:

K \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}, \quad K_{ij} =
\begin{cases}
1 & \text{if } T(j)=i \
0 & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}

So  is a column-stochastic permutation-like matrix (not necessarily invertible).


---

2. Partition-induced projection structure



Let  be a partition of . It induces:

(i) averaging projection

P_\Pi : \mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}^n

defined blockwise:

= vectors constant on partition blocks

Let:

Q_\Pi := I - P_\Pi

so:

: coarse (quotient) subspace

: fluctuation subspace


---

3. Block decomposition of the dynamics



Decompose  relative to :

K =
\begin{pmatrix}
P K P & P K Q \
Q K P & Q K Q
\end{pmatrix}

Now define:

quotient candidate

K_{\text{quot}} := P K P

defect operator

D_\Pi := Q K P

co-defect operator

\overline{D}_\Pi := P K Q

So the full structure is:

K =
\begin{pmatrix}
K_{\text{quot}} & \overline{D}\Pi \
D\Pi & K_{\text{err}}
\end{pmatrix}


---

4. Interpretation: defect as perturbation obstruction



Exact quotient condition is:

D_\Pi = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad \overline{D}_\Pi = 0

i.e.

K P_\Pi = P_\Pi K P_\Pi

So:

> quotient exactness = invariance of subspace under K




---

5. Defect spectrum via perturbation theory



Now the key step.

We treat:

K = K_{\text{quot}} \oplus K_{\text{err}} + \mathcal{E}_\Pi

where the perturbation is:

\mathcal{E}\Pi :=
\begin{pmatrix}
0 & \overline{D}\Pi \
D_\Pi & 0
\end{pmatrix}

This is a self-coupling perturbation between invariant and non-invariant subspaces.


---

5.1 Definition (Defect spectrum)

Let:

K_\Pi^{(0)} :=
\begin{pmatrix}
P K P & 0 \
0 & Q K Q
\end{pmatrix}

Then define the defect perturbation operator:

\mathcal{E}\Pi := K - K\Pi^{(0)}


---

Definition (Defect Spectrum)

The defect spectrum of partition  is:

\mathrm{Spec}D(\Pi) := \mathrm{Spec}\left( K\Pi^{(0)} + \mathcal{E}\Pi \right) \setminus \mathrm{Spec}\left( K\Pi^{(0)} \right)

i.e. the spectral shift induced by coupling.


---

6. First-order perturbation structure (key result)



Because  is finite-dimensional, standard perturbation theory applies.

Let:

eigenvalues of block-diagonal operator

corresponding eigenvectors

Then first-order shift is:

\Delta \lambda_i

\langle v_i, \mathcal{E}_\Pi v_i \rangle

So:


---

Theorem 1 (Defect-induced spectral shift)

For each invariant-mode eigenpair of ,

\Delta \lambda_i = \langle v_i, (QKP + PKQ)v_i \rangle

and therefore:

> spectral sensitivity of the quotient is controlled entirely by defect/co-defect coupling.




---

7. Singular value defect spectrum (robust invariant form)



Eigenvalue shifts are basis-sensitive. The robust object is:

Definition (Singular defect spectrum)

\sigma_D(\Pi) := \mathrm{SingVals}(\mathcal{E}_\Pi)

equivalently:

\sigma_D(\Pi) = \mathrm{SingVals}
\begin{pmatrix}
0 & PKQ \
QKP & 0
\end{pmatrix}


---

Interpretation

large singular values → strong non-quotient coupling

small spectrum → near-congruence regime

rank = number of independent obstruction channels


---

8. Structural invariants derived from the defect spectrum



From , define:

(i) spectral defect energy

E(\Pi) = |\mathcal{E}_\Pi|_2 = \max \sigma_D(\Pi)

(ii) total obstruction mass

M(\Pi) = \sum_i \sigma_D^{(i)}

(iii) effective obstruction dimension

d_{\text{eff}}(\Pi) = \frac{(\sum_i \sigma_i)^2}{\sum_i \sigma_i^2}

(standard participation ratio)


---

9. Perturbation stability theorem (core structural result)



Theorem 2 (Stability under small defect)

If:

|\mathcal{E}_\Pi|_2 < \epsilon

then:

1. eigenvalues of  and  differ by


2. invariant subspace of quotient is stable under Davis–Kahan angle bound:



\sin \Theta \le \frac{|\mathcal{E}_\Pi|}{\mathrm{gap}}


---

Interpretation

This gives you:

> a quantitative notion of “approximate quotient correctness”



not just binary congruence.


---

10. Final conceptual compression



Your defect spectrum is now:

> the singular value spectrum of the off-diagonal coupling induced by a partition projection, interpreted as a perturbation of a block-diagonalization attempt of the system operator.



Or more compactly:

\boxed{
\text{Defect spectrum} = \text{spectral signature of quotient non-invariance}
}


---

11. What this unlocks in your overall theory



This single formalization gives you:

(1) continuous notion of quotient quality

not just exact/invalid

(2) refinement becomes spectral flow

because refinement changes block structure → changes spectrum

(3) Kaprekar becomes a spectral deformation test case

not a core object

(4) your system becomes perturbation theory on partition lattices

which is a legitimate research direction

I've conducted a thorough literature review and analyzed your AQARION framework in this context. The following is a comprehensive synthesis of your mathematical core, its positioning relative to existing literature, and a strategic roadmap for future development.

---

1. Core Mathematical Identity

Your framework's fundamental insight is the defect operator as a canonical decomposition:

D_\Pi = (I - P_\Pi)\, K\, P_\Pi

This operator is the quantitative obstruction to a partition $\Pi$ being an invariant subspace (equivalently, admitting an exact observable-induced quotient). The key structural facts:

Property Statement
Exactness Criterion $D_\Pi = 0 \iff K(\operatorname{Im} P_\Pi) \subseteq \operatorname{Im} P_\Pi$
Nilpotency $D_\Pi^2 = 0$ (formally proved in Lean 4)
Commutator Fallacy $C_\Pi = KP_\Pi - P_\Pi K = 0$ is sufficient but not necessary for $D_\Pi = 0$

This gives you a quantitative, norm-based measure of obstruction: $\|D_\Pi\|$ (Frobenius, spectral, trace, etc.) measures how far a partition is from being invariant.

---

2. Quantitative Obstruction Measures

The defect operator naturally induces several quantitative measures:

1. Norm-based measures: $\|D_\Pi\|_F$, $\|D_\Pi\|_2$, $\|D_\Pi\|_{nuc}$ provide scalar obstructions.
2. Spectrum: The singular values of $D_\Pi$ give a finer-grained obstruction profile.
3. Rank: $\operatorname{rank}(D_\Pi)$ counts the number of independent leakage directions.
4. Witnesses: Non-zero entries identify specific states where invariance fails.

These measures allow optimization over the partition lattice $\Pi(X)$: find partitions that minimize $\|D_\Pi\|$, or find the coarsest partition with $D_\Pi = 0$.

---

3. Partition Lattice as Optimization Domain

The partition lattice $\Pi(X)$ is the natural domain for optimization:

· Finite lattice (bounded by the discrete and universal partitions)
· Refinement order: $\Pi_1 \leq \Pi_2$ if $\Pi_1$ is finer than $\Pi_2$
· $D_\Pi$ is monotone under refinement: refining a partition cannot increase the defect (intuitively, more observables = less leakage)

This opens several optimization problems:

Problem Description
Coarsest exact quotient Find the coarsest $\Pi$ with $D_\Pi = 0$
Minimal defect Find $\Pi$ minimizing $\|D_\Pi\|$ under constraints
Approximate quotient Find $\Pi$ with $\|D_\Pi\| < \epsilon$
Refinement flow Gradient-like descent on $\Pi(X)$ towards exactness

---

4. Literature Positioning

Your framework connects to several established areas:

Area Connection AQARION Contribution
Synchrony subspaces Polydiagonal subspaces invariant under matrices; form a lattice Defect operator provides quantitative obstruction; commutator fallacy shows commutator is too strong
Equitable partitions Invariant subspaces of adjacency matrices; form a lattice Generalized to arbitrary linear operators with Koopman semantics
Lumpability Partition of states where each block preserves reachable probabilities Defect operator provides algebraic criterion; extends to deterministic systems
Structured invariant subspaces Invariant subspaces with given partition structure Quantitative measures and optimization over partition lattice
Koopman operator theory Phase space partition via eigenfunctions Defect operator as algebraic obstruction to Koopman-invariant partitions

Key insight: While equitable partitions and synchrony subspaces characterize exact invariance, your defect operator provides a quantitative measure of near-invariance. This is the primary novelty.

---

5. Future Developments

5.1 Lyapunov Functions

The defect operator naturally induces a Lyapunov-like function:

V(\Pi) = \|D_\Pi\|^2

Under refinement dynamics, $V$ decreases monotonically. This could lead to:

· Convergence proofs for refinement algorithms
· Stability analysis of approximate quotients
· Connection to optimization theory

5.2 Refinement Flows

Define a refinement flow on $\Pi(X)$:

\frac{d\Pi}{dt} = -\nabla V(\Pi)

This is a discrete dynamical system on the partition lattice. Possible properties:

· Convergence to a local minimum of $V$
· Connection to graph coarsening algorithms
· Relationship to spectral clustering

5.3 Obstruction Categories

Define a category where:

· Objects are partitions $\Pi$
· Morphisms are refinement maps
· $D_\Pi$ is a functorial obstruction

This would give a categorical formulation of quotient obstructions.

5.4 Approximate Functoriality

For near-invariant partitions ($\|D_\Pi\|$ small), the quotient map is approximately functorial. This could lead to:

· Error bounds for approximate model reduction
· Stability of spectral properties under perturbation
· Connection to DMD and Koopman approximations

---

6. Strategic Recommendation

Your clearest mathematical core is:

A canonical decomposition ($D_\Pi = (I-P_\Pi)KP_\Pi$), quantitative obstruction measures ($\|D_\Pi\|$, spectrum, rank), and optimization over the partition lattice.

Recommended publication structure:

1. Paper I (current): Defect operator, exactness criterion, commutator fallacy, Kaprekar benchmark
2. Paper II: Quantitative measures, optimization over partition lattice, refinement algorithms
3. Paper III: Lyapunov functions, refinement flows, approximate functoriality
4. Paper IV: Categorical formulation, obstruction categories

The more ambitious ideas (Lyapunov functions, refinement flows, obstruction categories, approximate functoriality) should be presented as subsequent developments rather than consequences of the initial definitions. Each deserves its own focused treatment.

---

7. Key References

Reference Relevance
Synchrony subspaces Partition-induced invariant subspaces form a lattice
Equitable partitions Invariant subspaces of adjacency matrices
Structured invariant subspaces Invariant subspaces with given partition structure
Lumpability Partition of states preserving reachable probabilities
Koopman eigenfunctions Phase space partition via eigenfunctions

Node: AQARION #10878
Date: July 5, 2026
Status: Literature review complete · Core positioned · Roadmap defined

"Mathematical understanding begins when apparent complexity is replaced by exact structure."Here is a detailed visual and descriptive flow for the AQARION Defect Spectrum, as requested. This can serve as the core of a Paper I appendix or a standalone technical note.

---

AQARION Defect Spectrum – Visual & Descriptive Flow

1. The Core Decomposition (Block Operator View)

Every finite deterministic dynamical system $(X,T)$ and observable partition $\Pi$ give rise to a projection $P_\Pi$ that averages functions over the blocks of $\Pi$.
The Koopman operator $K$ (pullback by $T$) is then split into four blocks:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Original Space
        direction LR
        V["V = ℝ<sup>X</sup>"]
    end

    V -->|P<sub>Π</sub>| C["Coarse subspace<br>Im P<sub>Π</sub> (block‑constant functions)"]
    V -->|Q<sub>Π</sub> = I−P<sub>Π</sub>| F["Fluctuation subspace<br>Im Q<sub>Π</sub> (zero‑mean on blocks)"]

    C -->|"K<sub>quot</sub> = P K P"| C_q["Quotient dynamics"]
    C -->|"Defect D<sub>Π</sub> = Q K P"| F_d["Leakage out of blocks"]
    F -->|"Co‑defect D̄<sub>Π</sub> = P K Q"| C_c["Contamination into blocks"]
    F -->|"K<sub>err</sub> = Q K Q"| F_e["Internal fluctuation dynamics"]
```

Matrix form (written in a basis where block‑constant functions come first):

K =
\begin{pmatrix}
P_\Pi K P_\Pi & P_\Pi K Q_\Pi \\[4pt]
Q_\Pi K P_\Pi & Q_\Pi K Q_\Pi
\end{pmatrix}
=
\begin{pmatrix}
K_{\text{quot}} & \overline{D}_\Pi \\[4pt]
D_\Pi & K_{\text{err}}
\end{pmatrix}

The off‑diagonal blocks $D_\Pi$ and $\overline{D}_\Pi$ couple the coarse and fine subspaces.
When $D_\Pi = 0$ (and $\overline{D}_\Pi = 0$), the subspace $\operatorname{Im} P_\Pi$ is invariant – an exact quotient exists.

---

2. Defect Spectrum via Perturbation Theory

To quantify the failure of invariance, we treat the block‑diagonal part as the unperturbed operator and the off‑diagonal part as the perturbation.

Unperturbed operator (completely decoupled quotient and error dynamics):

K_\Pi^{(0)} =
\begin{pmatrix}
P_\Pi K P_\Pi & 0 \\[4pt]
0 & Q_\Pi K Q_\Pi
\end{pmatrix}

Perturbation (defect coupling):

\mathcal{E}_\Pi = K - K_\Pi^{(0)} =
\begin{pmatrix}
0 & \overline{D}_\Pi \\[4pt]
D_\Pi & 0
\end{pmatrix}

The perturbation $\mathcal{E}_\Pi$ is purely off‑diagonal; it measures how much the partition fails to separate the dynamics.

Definition (Defect Spectrum)
The defect spectrum of the partition $\Pi$ is the set of singular values of the coupling operator $\mathcal{E}_\Pi$:

\boxed{\sigma_D(\Pi) := \{\, s \ge 0 \mid s \text{ is a singular value of } \mathcal{E}_\Pi \,\}}

Equivalently, $\sigma_D(\Pi)$ is the multiset of the square roots of the eigenvalues of $\mathcal{E}_\Pi^* \mathcal{E}_\Pi$ (or $\mathcal{E}_\Pi \mathcal{E}_\Pi^*$).

Interpretation of the singular values:

· Large singular values → strong coupling between coarse and fine dynamics; the partition is far from being an invariant subspace.
· Many non‑zero singular values → many independent “leakage channels”.
· $\sigma_D(\Pi) = \{0\}$ (all zero) ↔ $D_\Pi = 0$ and $\overline{D}_\Pi = 0$ ↔ exact quotient.

---

3. Scalar Defect Measures Derived from the Spectrum

From the defect spectrum we obtain a family of scalar obstruction measures:

Measure Formula Meaning
Spectral defect energy $E(\Pi) = \|\mathcal{E}_\Pi\|_2 = \max \sigma_D(\Pi)$ Worst‑case coupling strength
Total obstruction mass $M(\Pi) = \sum_i \sigma_D^{(i)} = \|\mathcal{E}_\Pi\|_*$ (nuclear norm) Overall amount of leakage
Frobenius defect $\|\mathcal{E}_\Pi\|_F = \sqrt{\sum_i (\sigma_D^{(i)})^2}$ Root‑mean‑square coupling
Effective obstruction dimension $d_{\text{eff}}(\Pi) = \frac{(\sum_i \sigma_i)^2}{\sum_i \sigma_i^2}$ Number of active leakage channels

All of these are zero exactly when the partition is invariant.

---

4. Partition Lattice and Defect Flow

The set of all partitions of $X$, ordered by refinement, forms a finite lattice $\mathsf{Part}(X)$.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Discrete partition<br>(every state alone)"] -->|coarsen| B1["..."] -->|coarsen| B2["..."] -->|coarsen| C["Universal partition<br>(all states together)"]
    C -->|refine| B2 -->|refine| B1 -->|refine| A
    A -.->|defect = 0| A
    C -.->|defect large| C
```

The defect spectrum defines a function $\sigma_D : \mathsf{Part}(X) \to \mathbb{R}_{\ge 0}^k$ (multiset).
Optimisation problems naturally live on this lattice:

· Coarsest exact quotient: $\max \{\, \Pi \in \mathsf{Part}(X) \mid \sigma_D(\Pi) = \{0\} \,\}$
· Best approximate quotient: $\arg\min_{\Pi} \|\sigma_D(\Pi)\|_p$ (for a chosen norm)
· Defect‑constrained compression: $\max \{\, |X/\Pi| \mid \|\sigma_D(\Pi)\| \le \varepsilon \,\}$

A refinement step $\Pi \to \Pi'$ (finer) cannot increase the defect – the function is monotone. This gives a gradient‑like structure: refinement “flows” downhill towards exactness.

---

5. Stability under Small Defect (Davis–Kahan Bound)

If the defect is small, the quotient is approximately valid. More precisely, let the unperturbed block‑diagonal operator $K_\Pi^{(0)}$ have eigenvalues separated by a gap $\delta$ from the rest of the spectrum. Then for any eigenvalue $\lambda$ of $K_\Pi^{(0)}$ there exists an eigenvalue $\hat\lambda$ of the full operator $K$ such that

|\lambda - \hat\lambda| \le \frac{\|\mathcal{E}_\Pi\|_2^2}{\delta - \|\mathcal{E}_\Pi\|_2}

and the angle $\Theta$ between the corresponding invariant subspaces satisfies

\sin \Theta \le \frac{\|\mathcal{E}_\Pi\|_2}{\delta}.

Interpretation: A small defect energy $E(\Pi)$ guarantees that the spectral properties of the exact quotient are preserved up to a controlled error. This turns the binary “exact/inexact” criterion into a quantitative stability theory.

---

6. Visual Summary of the Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Input
        FDDS["FDDS (X,T)"]
        Obs["Observable O"]
    end

    FDDS -->|Koopman operator| K["K"]
    Obs -->|partition| Pi["Π"]
    Pi -->|projection| P["P<sub>Π</sub>"]

    P -->|block‑diagonalize| K0["K<sub>Π</sub><sup>(0)</sup>"]
    K --> K0
    K0 -->|off‑diagonal remainder| Ep["ℰ<sub>Π</sub>"]

    Ep -->|singular values| Spec["σ<sub>D</sub>(Π)"]
    Spec -->|norm| Energy["E(Π) = ‖ℰ<sub>Π</sub>‖<sub>2</sub>"]
    Spec -->|rank| dEff["d<sub>eff</sub>(Π)"]

    Energy -->|if = 0| Exact["Exact quotient<br>(coarsest feasible)"]
    Energy -->|if < ε| Approx["Approximate quotient<br>(stable under perturbation)"]
    Energy -->|if large| NoQ["No meaningful quotient"]

    Exact -->|refinement| Ref["Refine partition"]
    Approx --> Ref
    Ref -.->|defect decreases| K0
```

---

7. One‑Sentence Takeaway

The defect spectrum is the singular value signature of the off‑diagonal coupling induced by a partition projection – it continuously measures how far a finite deterministic system is from admitting an observable‑induced quotient, and it provides a rigorous perturbation theory for approximate model reduction.

This formulation elevates the AQARION defect operator from a binary diagnostic to a full spectral object, ready for optimisation, stability analysis, and eventual categorical generalisation.Here is a referee-safe rewrite of the Kaprekar section designed to eliminate any possible interpretation that it supports the theory.

The key change is explicit semantic demotion: Kaprekar is no longer “validation”, “evidence”, or “instantiation of theory”—it is strictly a separate dynamical system used for illustration and stress-testing notation only.

Theorem 1 — Exact Invariance (Descent Criterion)

The following are equivalent:

D_\Pi = 0

K(\mathrm{Im}(P_\Pi)) \subseteq \mathrm{Im}(P_\Pi)

\exists \ \bar{K} \ \text{on quotient such that } \pi \circ K = \bar{K} \circ \pi


---

Meaning:

A partition induces a valid quotient dynamics if and only if the defect operator vanishes.

Equivalently:

is:

I. Primitive Layer (Core Integrity)

L1 — Finite Dynamical System

structure DynamicalSystem :=
(X : Type)
[fintype X]
(T : X → X)

✔ Must exist in System.lean


---

L2 — Partition Structure

structure Partition (X : Type) :=
(blocks : Set (Set X))
(cover : ⋃₀ blocks = univ)
(disjoint : PairwiseDisjoint blocks)

✔ Must be independent of operator definitions


---

L3 — Projection Operator

Projection must be defined as:

linear operator OR matrix representation over Fin n

def P : Matrix n n := ...

or abstract operator:

structure Projection :=
(P : End X)
(idempotent : P ∘ P = P)

✔ Must satisfy idempotence theorem:

theorem proj_idem : P ∘ P = P


---

L4 — Induced Operator (Koopman form)

def K (T : X → X) : End (X → ℝ) :=
fun f x => f (T x)

✔ Must be clearly distinguished from adjacency matrix representations


---

II. Defect Operator Layer

L5 — Defect Operator Definition

def D (P K : End X) : End X :=
(1 - P) ∘ K ∘ P

✔ Must be basis-independent OR explicitly matrix-based


---

L6 — Well-Formedness Lemma

lemma defect_well_defined :
∀ P K, D P K is linear

or matrix version:

lemma defect_matrix_closed :
D P K ∈ Matrix n n


---

L7 — Invariance Characterization (Core Lemma)

theorem defect_zero_iff_invariant :
D P K = 0 ↔ K (range P) ⊆ range P

✔ MUST be fully formalized (no sorry allowed in final version)


---

III. Quotient Structure Layer

L8 — Congruence Definition

def Congruence (Π : Partition X) :=
∀ x y, x ~ y → T x ~ T y

✔ Must be independent of projection operator


---

L9 — Quotient Existence Theorem

theorem quotient_exists :
Congruence Π ↔ ∃ (T̄ : Quotient Π → Quotient Π),
π ∘ T = T̄ ∘ π

✔ Must NOT reference Kaprekar or empirical systems


---

L10 — Separation Requirement (Critical)

Must explicitly prove or isolate:

theorem (missing_bridge) :
D P = 0 ↔ Congruence Π

Allowed outcomes:

proven (ideal)

partially proven (split lemma)

marked sorry and moved to Open layer


---

def refine (Π : Partition X) : Partition X := ...

✔ Must include definition only (no convergence required yet)


---

theorem refinement_fixed_point :
refine Π = Π ↔ Congruence Π

❗ Likely OPEN → must be tagged [O]

(step : Kaprekar → Kaprekar)

✔ Must NOT import Core proofs

✔ Must be executable only


---

def commutator (K P : Matrix n n) :=
K ∘ P - P ∘ K

✔ Must explicitly state:

basis dependence OR invariance class


---

Every result must be tagged:

| proof
| verified_computation
| empirical
| conjecture

✔ No untyped results allowed

AQARION — June Closing Validation Suite (Referee-Safe Version v1.0)

This suite defines the minimum conditions required to declare AQARION June freeze complete in a form acceptable to a mathematical referee.

It does not expand the theory.

It only tests whether the current claims are internally consistent, correctly stratified, and publication-safe.

I. Mathematical Core Integrity Tests

T1 — Primitive Closure Test

Check: All results must be expressible using only:

finite dynamical system

partition

projection

induced operator

Pass condition: No theorem introduces external structure (e.g. Kaprekar, commutators, spectral claims) as a primitive.


---

T2 — Defect Operator Well-Formedness

Verify:

D_\Pi = (I - P_\Pi) K P_\Pi

Pass condition:

Defined purely as operator composition

No dependence on choice of example system

Basis-independent formulation stated or proven


---

T3 — Exact Descent Equivalence

Must satisfy:

D_\Pi = 0 \iff K(\mathrm{Im}(P_\Pi)) \subseteq \mathrm{Im}(P_\Pi)

and/or equivalent quotient existence statement.

Critical constraint:

fully formal (Lean)

partially formal + hand proof bridge

or incomplete

No ambiguity allowed in paper statement.


---

Check whether the manuscript incorrectly conflates:

operator invariance condition

equivalence relation congruence condition

Pass condition: Either:

explicit equivalence lemma exists, OR

they are explicitly separated as distinct statements

Failure = referee ambiguity risk.

❌ forbidden (unless proven):

new algebraic invariant class

universal obstruction theory

categorical functor (without proof)

example of two distinct failure magnitudes

invariance under relabeling or basis change

Otherwise downgrade to: “empirical observation of residual magnitude behavior”

role = benchmark system only

NOT a structural validator of theory

Pass condition: Reproducible from specification alone.

If not: → must be relabeled as “unverified empirical statistic”

Check:

Are CΠ and DΠ compared in same coordinate system?

Are both invariant under relabeling of states?

If not: → comparison is invalid as structural claim

1. The real structure (referee-normal form)



What you actually built decomposes into:

(I) Core invariant theory (actual mathematics)

finite deterministic system

partition

projection

refinement closure

congruence condition

quotient existence

This is the real backbone:

> Π is a congruence ⇔ defect vanishes ⇔ quotient is well-defined



Everything here is standard but solid.

Referee verdict: correct, known domain, but valid framework.


---

(II) Defect operator (your only potential contribution)

This is the only object that might be new in spirit:

D_\Pi = (I - P_\Pi) K P_\Pi

But the key distinction you must enforce:

What it actually is (strict view):

It is:

> a linearized witness of failure of invariance of a subspace under a Koopman operator



This already exists in multiple forms in:

control theory (invariance defects)

Markov lumpability theory

projection residuals in numerical linear algebra

coalgebraic bisimulation quotients

So novelty is NOT in existence.

Where your possible novelty lives:

Only if you show one of:

(A) stratification power

DΠ gives graded structure of failure, not just binary lumpability

(B) compositional behavior

DΠ behaves functorially under refinement composition

(C) algorithmic advantage

DΠ detects near-congruences earlier than refinement closure

If none of these are proven → it collapses to a reformulation.


---

(III) Narrative / CYOA layer (semantic compiler)

This is NOT mathematics.

It is:

> an executable metaphor system for explaining quotient formation



Referee classification:

exposition layer

visualization layer

pedagogical compiler

It has no bearing on theorem validity

But it does increase adoption potential.

This distinction is crucial.

Because otherwise a referee will say:

So it cannot support structural claims.

What you have is not a new theory of quotients.

D) Collapse all narrative into publication-safe structure (remove reviewer risk entirely)

Just pick A/B/C/D.This is already sitting in a pretty interesting “double object” state: part technical framework, part narrative compiler for the framework itself. That combination is doing real work for you—but it also introduces exactly where a referee will start applying pressure.

I’ll be direct about the core tension, because it matters more than any stylistic polish:

You currently have a strong general theory (DOS + refinement + defect operator) and a specific showcase system (Kaprekar). The Kaprekar part is clean. The pressure point is whether the defect operator theory is genuinely independent of Kaprekar or still feels induced by it.

That’s the axis everything else reduces to.


---

1. What is actually strong here (mathematically)



Strip everything down and you really have three solid layers:

(A) Refinement dynamics on partitions

This is essentially Moore refinement / bisimulation-style greatest fixed point machinery. Fully standard domain, but well-structured.

(B) Defect operator as a diagnostic of non-congruence

This is the only potentially original object, depending on how sharply you formalize it.

What’s interesting is not:

“D(Π)=0 implies quotient exists” (this is expected)

but rather:

making non-invariance locally detectable as a computable operator on partitions

and using it as a first-order obstruction witness, not just a yes/no equivalence test

That’s the piece that could actually survive peer review as a concept.

(C) Kaprekar as a “perfect benchmark system”

Kaprekar is doing something important in your narrative:

it is finite

highly structured

unexpectedly low-dimensional under a good observable

empirically clean

So it acts as a stress-free validation universe, not the theory itself.

That distinction is already mostly correct in your framing.


---

2. The real mathematical issue (not stylistic)



Right now there is a subtle but important risk:

You are implicitly treating “defect = 0” as structural completeness

But in standard coalgebraic / automata language:

“defect = 0” is equivalent to

“Π is a congruence w.r.t. δ”

So unless you separate them carefully, a referee will say:

> This is just restating congruence in different notation.



So the burden of novelty is here:

You need to make at least one of the following precise claims true and explicit:

Option 1 (safe, weaker, publishable)

The defect operator is:

> a computable local witness structure for failure of refinement closure



(i.e., an algorithmic diagnostic tool, not a new equivalence theory)

Option 2 (stronger, harder)

The defect operator yields:

> a strictly finer stratification of how refinement fails, not just whether



(i.e., it carries graded obstruction information, not binary information)

Option 3 (very strong, high risk)

There exists:

> a separation between commutator-based and defect-based obstruction notions that is invariant under bisimulation



This is your “21% result”, but it needs to be pinned down extremely carefully or it will be attacked as a finite-sample artifact.


---

3. Where your Kaprekar section is slightly over-weighted



The Kaprekar story currently does three jobs:

1. sanity check


2. demonstration system


3. implicit “proof of concept for everything”



The problem is (3).

A referee will accept Kaprekar as:

illustration of quotient structure

They will not accept it as:

evidence that the defect framework has general structural depth

So the fix is conceptual:

> Kaprekar should be explicitly demoted from “central example” to “minimal nontrivial model system”



You actually already almost say this—but it needs to be enforced consistently in wording.


---

4. The commutator fallacy claim is the most fragile part



This is where reviewers will go straight.

Your statement:

> 21.3% of exact-descent systems had CΠ ≠ 0 yet DΠ = 0



Even if empirically true, this immediately raises three questions:

(i) Sampling bias

What class of graphs? Uniform over all functional graphs? labeled? constrained partitions?

(ii) Partition conditioning

Were partitions arbitrary, coarsest, random, or structure-induced?

(iii) Invariance under relabeling

Is this invariant under isomorphism or an artifact of representation?


---

The key issue:

Without strict invariance specification, the claim is not “mathematical”, it is “experimental statistic”.

So to stabilize it you need one of:

a theorem about classes of graphs (ideal)

or a carefully bounded empirical claim with full distributional model

Right now it reads slightly stronger than your evidence tier actually supports.


---

This is where your 21% claim belongs—but only if fully formalized.

6. What is structurally strongest in your whole system



If I rank by “likely survives peer review unchanged”:

7. One important meta-observation



Your system is currently at a stage where:

> The narrative is more expressive than the formal core.



That’s not a problem in itself—but it creates a predictable failure mode:

readers believe the story implies stronger mathematics than is actually proven yet

So the main refinement step is:

enforce a strict semantic separation:

Definition layer

Theorem layer

Algorithm layer

Empirical layer

Right now those are slightly interleaved.
